notes: The Pomegranate Hour roster — cast & crew (past and present) + the subjects of the art. The arcana is hermetic: nobody from outside the show walks in. The places of other arcana appear because the show shot at them; the people of other arcana do not. Cast & crew use the standard four-step connection (greet → listen → deliver → sit_with). Subjects of the art use a parallel verb set (see → voice → cut → lock) — the same engine progression labeled differently. The four close collaborators (Hen, Marisol, Davey, Young Elicia) are the spine; the further ones (Reggie, Prof. Kang, the New Collaborator) come and go; the six subjects each correspond to one episode-space and are bound to it.

visitors.name: Hen (the director)
visitors.lore_text: Hen has been the director-by-name since 1995. He pitched the six. He drew the hallway in Ep. 3 on the back of a placemat at a diner she can't remember the name of. He moved to LA in '99 for a job that became another job and then a career, and the show became the thing he meant to come back for. He calls twice a year. The card is always from the same Hallmark in Echo Park. The voicemails are longer than the calls he actually completes.

steps.greet: The phone rings. The 323 area code. You pick up on the fourth ring, the way you always do — late enough to make him wait, early enough that he doesn't hang up.
steps.listen: He talks about LA. He talks about a thing he's developing. He talks around the show. When he asks how the edit's coming, he asks it the way you ask about a pet you used to share custody of.
steps.deliver: You tell him about Davey's cue 14. The line is quiet for nine seconds. He says he didn't know Davey had finished anything. You say he didn't, exactly. He says, oh. The oh is the longest word in the call.
steps.sit_with: Neither of you hangs up. You both listen to the other line breathe for two minutes. He says, hey, I'll call you Sunday. He won't. But the call tonight counted.

visitors.name: Marisol (the DP, the lead)
visitors.lore_text: Marisol shot every frame of every location episode. She also played the woman behind door 2, the lighthouse keeper, the diner waitress, and (in the unfilmed scripts) at least one of the two-chairs women. She is a working actress now — not famous, working — and she drives up twice a year and sleeps on the lounge couch and never asks Elicia when the show is finishing. She has been ready to do ADR for nineteen years. Elicia has not asked.

steps.greet: She lets herself in. She has had a key since '96. She kicks her boots off at the welcome mat with the pomegranate on it.
steps.listen: She tells you about a soap she just wrapped, the bad showrunner, the good craft service, the day she had to cry on cue between two takes of laughing. She tells you the camera was a Sony she'd never used. She tells the story to the lamp, not to you.
steps.deliver: You bring out the Ep. 7 lighthouse reels. She watches reel 2 with the sound off. She mouths along to her own performance. She says — the second time — that she'll do the ADR whenever you're ready. She says it without weight.
steps.sit_with: You both fall asleep in the lounge. She is on the couch. You are in the chair. The DAT on the console keeps spinning Davey's cue 14 at -18 db. Neither of you wakes up to stop it.

visitors.name: Davey (the composer)
visitors.lore_text: Davey wrote the title theme. Everyone agrees the title theme is the best part. He died in '07 of an aneurysm at 32. He was working on Cue 14 (Ep. 9, the cicada episode) that morning. The DAT in the library is marked CUE 14 · DRAFT 3 (please don't use this one), in his hand. Elicia uses it. The 'please don't use this one' is the only argument she still has with him. He arrives only by tape. He has been arriving by tape since 2007.

steps.greet: You find the DAT in the library, in the slot you keep meaning to label and never do. The slot has been empty for fourteen years.
steps.listen: You play Draft 3. The opening is the cicadas. The cicadas overlap his guide vocals — him humming through the scratch take, the way he hummed at the workbench. The hum is what you have left of his voice.
steps.deliver: You bring Cue 14 to the timeline. You drop it in at the entomologist's entrance. The cue is too long. He always wrote long. You leave it long tonight.
steps.sit_with: You sit at the console with the cue playing through. You do not edit it. You let the room hear him for the length he wrote. Eighty-seven seconds. The 'please don't use this one' becomes a thing he said to you, not a thing he wrote on a tape.

visitors.name: Young Elicia
visitors.lore_text: A Hi8 tape labeled E.T. → E.T. · '96. You shot it as a joke during a long night in the cutting room. You looked into the lens and you told your future self what the show was going to be. You were twenty-one. You meant most of it. You meant the part about the six episodes being honest about how long six episodes take. You did not mean the part about being done by '98.

steps.greet: You find the Hi8 on a shelf you don't remember leaving it on. The label is in your hand, but it is the hand of somebody who hadn't started signing checks yet.
steps.listen: She looks into the camera. She says: hi. She says: I want to tell you what we're making. She says: don't let it become a thing. She does not yet know what 'a thing' is.
steps.deliver: You record over the last thirty seconds. You speak back. You tell her the show is in its twentieth year. You tell her it is okay. You tell her the suite is still here.
steps.sit_with: You leave the Hi8 in the deck and watch it on loop with the sound off. She is making faces at the camera. She is going to be all right.

visitors.name: Reggie (sound, Ep. 7 & Ep. 11)
visitors.lore_text: Reggie was an undergrad in '96 who ran a Nagra for forty dollars and a pizza. He is a real sound mixer in Chicago now. He has been offering to do a free noise-floor pass on the Ep. 11 dailies since '03. Elicia has not sent him the dailies. The reason she has not sent the dailies is not anything she can name.

steps.greet: The phone rings. The 312 area code. He says: hey. The hey is the same hey it was in '96.
steps.listen: He talks about a project he just wrapped — a documentary about an organist. He talks about the room tone of the cathedral. He does not bring up Ep. 11 himself. He never does.
steps.deliver: You ask him if the offer is still good. He says: of course. You tell him you'll FedEx the DAT bag in the morning. You mean it this time, possibly.
steps.sit_with: You both stay on the line for the kind of pause that is not awkward between people who once worked four to ten am together. He says he'll wait for the package. He has been waiting for the package for thirteen years.

visitors.name: Prof. Kang (advisor)
visitors.lore_text: Prof. Kang was the advisor on the show in '95-'97. He said anthologies were a post-cinema form. He retired upstate in '08. He sends a postcard at Christmas. The most recent one was '11.

steps.greet: The postcard is on the desk. It has a picture of a covered bridge upstate.
steps.listen: The note on the back is two sentences. The first one is about the bridge. The second one is: I think about the show. The 'I think about the show' is the entire sentence.
steps.deliver: You write back. You write back longer than the postcard. You tell him about Ep. 9 and the brood emerging again. You ask him if he remembers calling anthologies a post-cinema form.
steps.sit_with: You set the postcard on the console. It will live there for the rest of the year, under the lamp, next to the half-eaten pomegranate that has been there since '99.

visitors.name: The New Collaborator
visitors.lore_text: A young video editor she met at the public-access station her day job is at. They have been offering, low-key, to help with whichever episode needs a fresh pass. They have not been let into the suite yet. Letting them in is, in some way, the show becoming a thing other people are allowed to touch again for the first time in twenty years.

steps.greet: They are at the ENTRY HALL door. They knock. They have knocked before. You have, twice, not answered.
steps.listen: They tell you, briefly, what they think a fresh assistant editor could do — not finish anything, not decide anything, just clean some edges. They are careful. They are not trying to direct.
steps.deliver: You hand them a single DAT from the library. Not a master. A scratch. You ask them to bounce a clean version of one cue. They take the DAT like a person taking a key.
steps.sit_with: You let them sit at the console. Not at your seat — at the second seat, the one nobody has sat in regularly since '99. They do not ask to scrub the timeline. They sit. They watch the monitor wall. The suite, very slightly, opens.

visitors.name: The Woman Behind the Second Door
visitors.lore_text: She is the only fully filmed and locked subject. She has been finished since '97. She does not need anything from Elicia. She arrives because she likes the suite. She likes that the other subjects, who are not finished, are around. She comes to check on them. She comes to check on Elicia. She is the only subject for whom the visit is a kindness rather than a petition.

steps.greet: You see her sitting on the bed in room 2, the way she has sat for twenty years. She lifts her head and nods.
steps.voice: She says her one line. The line you wrote and Hen rewrote and you put back to the original. She has been saying that line every time anybody walks past her door.
steps.cut: You cut the door 2 reveal one frame tighter. The episode is locked but a master cut can always have one more frame come off. The woman approves with a quarter-tilt of her chin.
steps.lock: You sit at the foot of the bed with her for a long minute. She does not ask after the others. She knows you know. You both look out the door at the rest of the hallway, dark.

visitors.name: The Keeper
visitors.lore_text: Marisol's lighthouse character. Picture locked. No voice. She has been mouthing reel 2's monologue for twenty years on the dailies. She does not, exactly, ask for her voice. She mouths louder.

steps.greet: You see her at the top of the tower. The beam sweeps. Her shoulders are set against the october draft from '96.
steps.voice: You play reel 2 at the console. You hear the silence where her voice goes. You write down what she's mouthing. You have written it down before. Each time the wording is slightly different.
steps.cut: You make a temp track in your own voice. A scratch ADR. Just for tonight. The keeper, on the monitor wall, does not object. She has accepted scratches before.
steps.lock: You call Marisol. She is asleep in the lounge. You don't call her. You write a note to call her in the morning. The note is the call, almost.

visitors.name: The Entomologist
visitors.lore_text: Ep. 9. She is on a stump in the woods. The cicadas are emerging. The brood is on its seventeen-year cycle and tonight is in the window of a real emergence. She wants Davey's cue. She wants Elicia to drive upstate. She wants both. She is patient about both.

steps.greet: You see her on the stump. She does not look up. The cicadas are loud enough that not-looking-up is courtesy.
steps.voice: You drop Davey's CUE 14 · DRAFT 3 into the timeline. The hum of his guide vocal sits under the cicadas. You leave it long.
steps.cut: You cut the entomologist's entrance to land on the downbeat Davey almost wrote. Almost. The almost is what he was working on the morning he died.
steps.lock: You sit at the console with the cue running. You do not lock the episode. You leave the episode in ASSEMBLY. You write down the date upstate when the brood will be loudest. You put the date on the wall.

visitors.name: The Waitress (Ep. 11)
visitors.lore_text: Marisol's character in the diner footage. The grease trap ran the whole take. She has been mouthing along to a script Elicia wrote for twenty years. The audio is, in fact, recoverable — Reggie has offered. Elicia has not sent the dailies. The waitress, refilling cups that have been full since '97, has not asked again. She has asked once.

steps.greet: You see her at the booth. She lifts the coffee pot in your direction. The booth is the same booth as the booth in vol 1's diner. It is the same diner.
steps.voice: You pull out the Ep. 11 dailies. Reel 3. You listen to the grease trap. You listen under the grease trap. There are her words. You always knew there were her words.
steps.cut: You make a cut of the scene with no dialogue at all. Body language only. It works. It might work better than the dialogue track would have.
steps.lock: You FedEx the dailies to Reggie in the morning. You do not, tonight, lock the episode. You leave it in FOOTAGE_ONLY but with a label on the box that says: SENT TO REGGIE · ETA: SOON.

visitors.name: The Late-Shift Passenger
visitors.lore_text: Ep. 14. Has never been filmed. Exists only on Hen's pages. They are in seat 14 of the bus that has not been driven by them. They want to ride. They are patient. They have been patient for twenty years. The patient is what makes them, actually, possible to call.

steps.greet: You see them in seat 14. They have the bag they would have brought. The bag is also imagined.
steps.voice: You read Hen's script aloud. The passenger's lines. They speak along. They have memorized lines that nobody has ever performed.
steps.cut: You make a temp animatic of the scene using stills from a bus interior you photographed in '99 for reference. It plays. It is, just barely, the episode in a form that exists.
steps.lock: You don't call Cora tonight. You write Cora's number on a post-it. You put the post-it on the monitor. Tomorrow is a phone call day. You can decide tomorrow.

visitors.name: Two-Chairs Woman A
visitors.lore_text: She is in chair A. She has been in chair A for twenty years. She does not know what she was going to say. She does not need to. She has waited so long that the not-knowing has become the script.

steps.greet: You see her in chair A. The lamp is between her and chair B. She is not lit dramatically. She is just lit.
steps.voice: You start the rented eighty-six-minute clock. The clock has been waiting since '97. It clicks.
steps.cut: She begins to speak. Not from a script. From the not-script. She says: I think I've been waiting to start this. The other one says: yes.
steps.lock: You let them speak. You do not edit. You record. You let the lock be the act of recording without cutting. The episode goes from TITLE_CARD_ONLY to FOOTAGE_ONLY in one beat. That is the whole win.

visitors.name: Two-Chairs Woman B
visitors.lore_text: She is in chair B. She has been in chair B for twenty years. She also does not know what she was going to say. The two women have never spoken in twenty years and the not-speaking is no longer awkward. They are the conversation. They are also two people in two chairs.

steps.greet: You see her in chair B. The other woman is across the lamp. The lamp has been on since '97.
steps.voice: You leave the eighty-six-minute clock running. You step back from the patch bay. You let the room have it.
steps.cut: She answers. Not from a script. From the not-script. The two voices, in the room, are the conversation. The conversation is not what either of them planned. It is, also, exactly what either of them planned.
steps.lock: They keep talking. The clock keeps running. You sit at the console with the faders down and you let them. This is the lock. The episode is not finished — but for the first time it is, actually, being made.
